content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY

591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

370 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

TELEPHONE (916) 446 6752

SAN FRANCISCO

650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

March 1, 1990

HAND DELIVERED

Ms. Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice - Independent Expenditures

Dear Ms. Donovan:

In the past few months, many clients of this firm have
asked what guidelines apply to making independent expenditures.
We are requesting formal advice pursuant to Government Code
section 83114(b) on behalf of three of those clients, each of
which is currently considering establishment of its own program
for making independent expenditures, as defined in Government
Code section 82031. Because the issues about which they are
concerned are similar, the following clients have joined in the
advice request:

•

The California Correctional Peace Officers
Association ("CCPOA"), a union of state
correctional peace officers whose 19,000 members
contribute on a voluntary basis to CCPOA's
sponsored Political Action Committee ("PAC"),
CCPOA-PAC;

•

California Professional Firefighters ("CPF"), a
labor organization comprised of approximately
17,000 professional firefighters, whose members
voluntarily contribute to CPF-PAC;

•

The California Medical Association ("CMA"), a
professional association of California physicians,
approximately 9,000 of whom have chosen to
contribute to California Medical PAC.

The committees sponsored by CCPOA, CPF, and CMA are funded
by contributions from union or association members. Contributors
to CCPOA-PAC and CPF-PAC contribute less than $100 each per
fiscal year; most physicians currently contribute less than $100
per fiscal year to California Medical PAC, and none contribute
more than $2,500 each per fiscal year. Based on its past
activity, each of the sponsored PACs mentioned above has
qualified as a "broad-based political committee" (Gov't Code

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 2


§ 85102(d)); each contributes primarily, if not exclusively, to
legislative candidates and candidates for statewide office.¹

     Neither CCPOA, CPF, nor CMA has in the past made
independent expenditures for or against a candidate, through its
sponsored PAC or otherwise. However, each organization is
currently considering establishment of its own independent
expenditure program.  Each is familiar with the general
guidelines regarding independent expenditures contained in your
letter to Assemblyman Richard Mountjoy, FPPC No. I-89-426.
CCPOA, CPF, and CMA now request formal advice from the Commission
regarding application of the general rules about independent
expenditures to the facts stated below.

     A.  Pre-Endorsement Contacts with Candidates

                              FACTS

     CCPOA has a long-established policy and practice that
candidates for the same office from each of the two major
political parties be interviewed by CCPOA-PAC (the seven
Executive Council members and two appointed members-at-large)
before any decision to endorse a candidate for that office is
made. Candidates are sent letters inviting them to participate
in the interview process. CCPOA does not mention possible
financial support of any candidate in the invitation letters.
The pre-endorsement interviews are usually held shortly after the
primary election.² At the interview, each candidate is asked a
series of questions, formulated by the CCPOA Board of Directors
(all the elected local chapter presidents), designed to elicit
the candidates' views on issues of importance to CCPOA members.
No questions are asked regarding the candidate's campaign
strategy or campaign funding needs, plans, or projects.

     After the interview process, CCPOA-PAC votes on which
candidates to endorse. The endorsement of particular candidates
is communicated to CCPOA members through The Peacekeeper, CCPOA's
monthly magazine. On occasion, a supplemental communication has

___________________________

     ¹ CCPOA-PAC, California Medical PAC, and CPF-PAC may
occasionally make insubstantial contributions to measure
committees from PAC funds; however, CMA and CPF usually make such
contributions through their sponsored issue committees.

     ² In a special election, the candidates are usually
interviewed prior to the first of the election dates. Not all
candidates may be invited, depending on the nature of the
district; however, an attempt is made to invite the leading
contenders and to be sure that candidates from both major parties
are included.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 3


been sent to members residing in a particular district.³
Following the endorsement process, the CCPOA-PAC board may decide
to give financial or other support to an endorsed candidate. In
the past, support has been in the form of contributions.

     CPF and CMA currently have a less structured candidate
interview process.⁴ However, each is considering establishing a
procedure similar to that used by CCPOA. Should they decide to
establish a more structured interview procedure, neither CPF nor
CMA (or their PAC representatives) would be asking questions of
candidates who appear about the candidates' campaign plans,
needs, strategy, or projects.

     CCPOA, CPF and CMA each strongly believe that
obtaining candidates' views on issues of significance to their membership is a fundamental to the
informed exercise of First Amendment speech and associational
rights. The interview is of particular importance as a means of
learning where non-incumbents stand, as their views on issues
not usually as widely-known as an incumbent's. However, CCPOA,
CPF, and CMA each understands that if a decision is made to make
an independent expenditure for a particular candidate following
an interview, they may not consult, coordinate, or act in concert
with the affected candidate or his or her controlled committee(s)
regarding that expenditure.


_______________________

     ³ CPF and CMA also have monthly magazines: California
Firefighter and California Physician, respectively. CPF, CCPOA,
and CMA understand that when independent expenditures are being
made in support of a candidate, any simultaneous communications
to members regarding an endorsement of the affected candidate
also need to be made independently of the candidate.
Customarily, the fact that CCPOA has endorsed a candidate has
been communicated also to the candidate, usually by a telephone
call from a PAC representative. CCPOA is requesting advice
regarding whether this practice may continue. See Question No.
2.

     ⁴ Both CMA and CPF have informal interview procedures. CPF
has delegated the power to endorse to its executive board, which
frequently interviews candidates (not necessarily from both
parties) in connection with an endorsement. The CPF executive
board informs CPF-PAC of the endorsement and CPF-PAC then decides
to which endorsed candidates to contribute. Within CMA,
candidate interviews are occasionally conducted by the local
county medical society, which then communicates the views of the
candidates to California Medical PAC and makes contribution
recommendations.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 4


QUESTIONS

Question No. 1: Under the circumstances described
above, if a candidate accepts an invitation to appear before
board representatives of CCPOA, CPF, CMA, or their sponsored PACs
and is interviewed exclusively about his or her views on issues
of significance to the organization's members (with no discussion
of the candidate's campaign plans, projects, or needs), may
CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their PACs subsequently make expenditures
above the amounts specified in Government Code section 85303(b)
in support of or opposition to that candidate, provided there is
no consultation, coordination or concerted act between the
expending organization (or its PAC) and the affected candidate
which occurs subsequent to the interview?

Question No. 2: If the answer to the above is
affirmative, may the interviewing organization also communicate
to a candidate who has been endorsed the fact of the endorsement
(provided the communication is restricted to conveying that
fact), and subsequently make expenditures in support of that
candidate greater than the amounts specified in Government Code
section 84303(b)?

B. Parameters of Consultation, Coordination, and
Concerted Activity

FACTS

CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC, and California Medical PAC have
each made contributions in the past to candidates they may now

_______________________
5 We are aware of technical advice issued to Judge Rutberg
(FPPC No. A-87-292) prior to the passage of Proposition 73.
Judge Rutberg was advised that expenditures made by an
organization by which he had been interviewed were reportable on
his campaign statement as contributions received by him, even
though Rutberg did not know of the timing, content, or cost
of the organization's slate mailer endorsing him (or even that he
had been endorsed) until he received a copy of the mailer through
regular mail delivery. However, in Rutberg, the organization
making the expenditure characterized it as an in-kind
contribution in a letter sent to the Judge after the election.
Judge Rutberg was also informed prior to the interview that a
possible slate mailer was "one form of publicity under
consideration" if the organization endorsed him. The Rutberg
letter appears to assume that by submitting to an interview on
issues knowing that a possible expenditure was "under
consideration", the candidate and the organization have
coordinated the expenditure.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 5


wish to support through an independent expenditures campaign.
Each organization is also actively considering retaining its own
professional campaign consultant to advise it regarding
independent expenditures.

     Assume that a decision is made to make independent
expenditures (above the amounts prescribed by Government Code
section 85303(b)) in support of a particular candidate, either
after the interview process described above or without the benefit
of that process if the answers to the above questions are
negative.

     Question No. 3: Do the following actions by CCPOA,
CPF, CMA, or their sponsored PACs preclude the organizations or
their PACs from subsequently making independent expenditures
supporting a particular candidate (or opposing his or her
opponent) in amounts above those specified in Government Code
section 85303(b)?

     a.  Having contributed in a prior fiscal year or a
         prior special election cycle to the candidate who
         would be supported in the expenditure?°

     b.  Having contributed to the campaign of the candidate
         for one office (i.e. Senate) in the same fiscal
         year as an expenditure would be made supporting
         that candidate for another office (i.e. Lt.
         Governor, Insurance Commissioner, etc.)?

     c.  Having contributed to the candidate who would be
         supported in the expenditure in the same fiscal
         year or special election cycle as the expenditure
         would be made?

     d.  Contracting with a consultant or pollster who in
         the past, but not in the current fiscal year or
         special election cycle, has received compensation
         from the affected candidate or his or her
         controlled committee?¹

__________________________

     ⁶ See In re Bell (1988) 11 FPPC Ops. 1, 18.

     ⁷ See 11 CFR section 109.1(b)(4)(i)(B), copy attached.
According to Mr. Brad Litchfield, of the Federal Election
Commission ("FEC") office of General Counsel, the FEC interprets
this provision to permit an independent committee to retain a
consultant who is not employed by the candidate in the current
federal election cycle but who may have worked for the candidate
in the past.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 6


        e.  Requesting and obtaining any of the following items
            from agents of the affected candidate's controlled
            committee (not from the candidate directly):8

            1) a glossy photograph(s) of the candidate;

            2) a copy of the candidate's official
               biography?

            3) a copy of the candidate's statement
               distributed to the press in connection with
               his or her announcement of candidacy?

            4) a copy of any other press release
               distributed by the affected candidate or
               his or her committee?

            5) a copy of the text of speeches made by the
               affected candidate?

            6) a copy of any of the affected candidate's
               position papers which have been published
               or distributed to the public?

            7) copies of press clippings about the
               affected candidate?

            8) polling data regarding the affected
               candidate which has been released to the
               press?

    C. Interaction with Parties, Other Candidates, and
       Other Committees

                              FACTS

        As broad-based committees which contribute to
    candidates, CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC and California Medical PAC each
    receive unsolicited materials seeking their support for a
    particular candidate. Recently, California Medical PAC has
    received telephone calls from member(s) of the legislature and
    party representatives about whether it intends to make
    independent expenditures in support of (or opposition to) a
    particular candidate(s). CPF received an unsolicited
    "promotional package" from the Republican legislative leadership

    ________________________
        8 In case of non-incumbents, the items listed in 1)-8) may
    not be available except through the candidate's campaign.   CCPOA,
    CPF, CMA and their sponsored PACs have also requested advice
    about unsolicited materials. See questions 4 through 8.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 7


in connection with the recent 27th A.D. special election,
containing brochures about of the candidate supported by the
leadership, biographical, polling, and demographic information,
etc. In connection with the run-off in the 27th A.D. (held on
January 30, 1990), CPF also received an unsolicited promotional
package containing similar materials about the Democratic
candidate, with a cover letter from four incumbent assembly
members.  CCPOA-PAC received similar materials, and has also
been asked generally about whether or not it intends to make
independent expenditures.  The general questions have not focused
on a particular candidate.

     California Medical PAC, CPF-PAC, and CCPOA-PAC have not
responded to these inquiries with any information about who (if
anyone) they intend to support or oppose through an independent
expenditure program.  They have declined to talk about the
subject.  However, each organization and its PAC is concerned
that, if they embark upon an independent expenditure program
(which always involves the design, printing, mailing, or
broadcasting of communications to voters), the independence of
the expenditure could be jeopardized by contacts they do not
initiate.  This could result not only in wasted time and money,
but in a frustration of the organization's right to speak out in
support of the affected candidate to the degree it wishes.

     In light of these facts, and of the language in
Government Code section 82031 that an expenditure is independent
if it is not made "to or at the behest of the affected
candidate," CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC, and California Medical PAC each
request advice about what role the legislative leadership and the
political parties may play in recommending to the PAC or
organization board, or representatives of either, that
independent expenditures be made in particular races in support
of or opposition to particular candidates.10

QUESTIONS

     Question 4:  If members of the legislature (including
the leadership or their agents make an unsolicited request of
CPF, CMA, CCPOA or their sponsored PACs to make independent

_______________________
     9 Challengers or non-incumbents running in open seats often
send these "promotional packages", unsolicited, to likely
supporters.  See Question 8.

     10 The organizations and their PACs presume that they can
rely on any information in the public domain, such as newspaper
articles, press releases, or other generally disseminated
information about which races have been "targeted" by the
leadership or the parties.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 8


expenditure(s) for (or against) a specific candidate (other than
themselves) are CPF, CCPOA, CMA or their PACs prohibited from
subsequently making expenditures for (or against) that candidate
in amounts above those specified in Government Code section
85303(b)?

Question 5: Would the answer to Question 4 be
different if the unsolicited request did not mention "independent
expenditures" as such but simply requested "support"?

Question 6: If political party leaders (or their
agents) make unsolicited requests (as described in questions 4
and 5), may CCPOA, CMA, CPF or their sponsored PACs subsequently
make expenditures for or against the candidate who is the subject
of the request in amounts over those prescribed in Government
Code section 85303(b)?

Questions 7: If CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their sponsored
PACs receive unsolicited "promotional materials" (of the type
described in Question 3 c.(1)-(8), see p. 6) from the legislative
leadership or the parties, may they use any or all of those
materials in their independent expenditure program?

Question 8: If CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their sponsored PACs
receive unsolicited "promotional materials" (of the same type as
above) from a candidate, may they use any or all of those
materials in their independent expenditure program?11

Question 9: Provided no one within any expending
committee is acting at the behest of an affected candidate, may
CPF, CCPOA, CMA or their sponsored PACs coordinate their
expenditures in support of or opposition to an affected candidate
with another political committee, broad-based political
committee, or person making expenditures for or against the same
candidate?

D. Volunteer Activity by Members

FACTS

As large organizations, CCPOA, CPF, and CMA have
individual members who independently volunteer their efforts in
political campaigns. CCPOA, CMA, and CPF each intend to
concentrate decision-making about the nature, content, timing,
design, and scope of any independent expenditure at the state board
level. However, such a board has no ability to direct and
control (or even be informed about) the actions of individual
correctional peace officers, firefighters, and doctors within

11
As noted earlier, non-incumbents often send these.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 9


their own districts. CCPOA, CMA, CPF and their PACs are
concerned about the possible impact of member volunteer activity
in a candidate's campaign on their ability to make independent
expenditures for that candidate.

QUESTION

Question 10: Assuming that no individual who
participates in the independent expenditure decision-making
process is also active in the affected candidate's campaign, if
other individual CCPOA, CPF, or CMA members volunteer in the
affected candidate's campaign, are CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their PACs
prohibited from making expenditures in support of that candidate
above the amounts specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

D. Timing and Reporting of Independent Expenditures

FACTS

CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their PACs are concerned that
before a mailing or other communication advocating the election
of a candidate is sent, circumstances might dictate that the
mailer be stopped or the broadcast cancelled. Also, the
organizations and their PACs are uncertain at what point in time
the independent expenditure notice required by 2 Cal. Code of
Regs. section 18564 is "triggered".

QUESTION

Question 11: Has an independent expenditure or in-kind
contribution for a mailing or other communication advocating the
election of a candidate been "made" for purposes of the limits
imposed by Government Code section 85303(c), the notice required
by section 85604, or reporting on the allocation schedule of a
campaign statement if no communication is actually sent or
broadcast?

E. Appropriateness of Federal Independent Expenditure
Guidelines

FACTS

Because 1990 is an election year, CCPOA, CPF, CMA and
their PACs will be making imminent decisions about their

_______________________

12 Of course, all expenditures to vendors made or incurred
in connection with the specified mailing would be reportable by
the PAC on Schedules E or F of its campaign statement.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
March 1, 1990
Page 10


independent expenditure programs.  Therefore, as a final question
they ask the following:

                              QUESTION

     Question 12:  While awaiting your response to this
letter and in the absence of regulations issued by the
Commission, may CPF, CCPOA, CMA and their sponsored PACs look to
the attached FEC regulation (11 CFR § 109.1) on independent
expenditures and the FEC Advisory Opinions interpreting it for
guidance?  We would appreciate an expedited response to this
final question.

     In light of impending special and primary elections, we
thank you in advance for your prompt consideration of these
particular questions.  Please do not hesitate to call me, Chuck
Bell, or Chip Nielsen if you need additional material facts or if
you need additional time to respond to this request for formal
advice.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]

                              JUDITH SPROUL DAVIS

JSD/kab
Attachment

cc:  Dan Terry, President
     California Professional Firefighters
     1780 Creekside Oaks, Suite 200
     Sacramento, CA 95833

     Donald L. Novey, President
     California Correctional Peace Officers Association PAC
     3780 Rosin Court, Suite 200
     Sacramento, CA 95834

     Allen Pross, Executive Director
     California Medical PAC
     P.O. Box 7690
     San Francisco, CA 95120-7690

§ 109.1 Definitions (2 U.S.C. 431(17)).

(a) "Independent expenditure" means an expenditure by a person for a communication expressly advocating the election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate which is not made with the cooperation or with the prior consent of, or in consultation with, or at the request or suggestion of, a candidate or any agent or authorized committee of such candidate.

Do For purposes of this definition—

(1) "Person" means an individual, partnership, committee, association, or any organization or group of persons, including a separate segregated fund established by a labor organization, corporation, or national bank (see Part 114) but does not mean a labor organization, corporation, or national bank.

(2) "Expressly advocating" means any communication containing a message advocating election or defeat, including but not limited to the name of the candidate, or expressions such as "vote for," "elect," "support," "cast your ballot for," and "Smith for Congress" or "vote against," "defeat," or "reject."

(3) "Clearly identified candidate" means that the name of the candidate appears, a photograph or drawing of the candidate appears, or the identity of the candidate is otherwise apparent by unambiguous reference.

(4) "Made with the cooperation or with the prior consent of, or in consultation with, or at the request or suggestion of, a candidate or any agent or authorized committee of the candidate" means—

(i) Any arrangement, coordination, or direction by the candidate or his or her agent prior to the publication, distribution, display, or broadcast of the communication. An expenditure will be presumed to be so made when it is—

(A) Based on information about the candidate's plans, projects, or needs provided to the expending person by the candidate, or by the candidate's agents, with a view toward having an expenditure made;

(B) Made by or through any person who is, or has been, authorized to raise or expend funds, who is, or has been, an officer of an authorized committee, or who is, or has been, receiving any form of compensation or reimbursement from the candidate, the candidate's committee or agent;

(ii) But does not include providing to the expending person upon request Commission guidelines on independent expenditures.

(5) "Agent" means any person who has actual oral or written authority, either express or implied, to make or to authorize the making of expenditures on behalf of a candidate, or means any person who has been placed in a position within the campaign organization where it would reasonably appear that in the ordinary course of campaign-related activities he or she may authorize expenditures.

(c) An expenditure not qualifying under this section as an independent expenditure shall be a contribution in-kind to the candidate and an expenditure by the candidate, unless otherwise exempted.

(d)(1) The financing of the dissemination, distribution, or republication in whole or in part, of any broadcast or any written, graphic, or other form of campaign materials prepared by the candidate, his campaign committee(s), or their authorized agents shall be considered a contribution for the purpose of contribution limitations and reporting responsibilities by the person making the expenditure but shall not be considered an expenditure by the candidate or his authorized committees unless made with the cooperation or operation with the prior consent of, or in consultation with, or at the request or suggestion of, a candidate or any authorized agent or committee thereof.

(2) This paragraph does not affect the right of a State or subordinate party committee to engage in such dissemination, distribution, or republication as agents designated by the national committee pursuant to § 110.7(a)(4).

(e) No expenditure by an authorized committee of a candidate on behalf of that candidate shall qualify as an independent expenditure.

sections.question:
Question No. 1: Under the circumstances described above, if a candidate accepts an invitation to appear before board representatives of CCPOA, CPF, CMA, or their sponsored PACs and is interviewed exclusively about his or her views on issues of significance to the organization's members (with no discussion of the candidate's campaign plans, projects, or needs), may CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their PACs subsequently make expenditures above the amounts specified in Government Code section 85303(b) in support of or opposition to that candidate, provided there is no consultation, coordination or concerted act between the expending organization (or its PAC) and the affected candidate which occurs subsequent to the interview?5

Question No. 2: If the answer to the above is affirmative, may the interviewing organization also communicate to a candidate who has been endorsed the fact of the endorsement (provided the communication is restricted to conveying that fact), and subsequently make expenditures in support of that candidate greater than the amounts specified in Government Code section 84303(b)?

B. Parameters of Consultation, Coordination, and Concerted Activity

FACTS

CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC, and California Medical PAC have each made contributions in the past to candidates they may now

5 We are aware of technical advice issued to Judge Rutberg (FPPC No. A-87-292) prior to the passage of Proposition 73. Judge Rutberg was advised that expenditures made by an organization by which he had been interviewed were reportable on his campaign statement as contributions received by him, even though Judge Rutberg did not know of the timing, content, or cost of the organization's slate mailer endorsing him (or even that he had been endorsed) until he received a copy of the mailer through regular mail delivery. However, in Rutberg, the organization making the expenditure characterized it as an in-kind contribution in a letter sent to the judge after the election. Judge Rutberg was also informed prior to the interview that a possible slate mailer was "one form of publicity under consideration" if the organization endorsed him. The Rutberg letter appears to assume that by submitting to an interview on issues knowing that a possible expenditure was "under consideration", the candidate and the organization have coordinated the expenditure.

wish to support through an independent expenditures campaign. Each organization is also actively considering retaining its own professional campaign consultant to advise it regarding independent expenditures.

Assume that a decision is made to make independent expenditures (above the amounts prescribed by Government Code section 85303(b)) in support of a particular candidate, either after the interview process described above or without the benefit of that process if the answers to the above questions are negative.

Question No. 3: Do the following actions by CCPOA, CPF, CMA, or their sponsored PACs preclude the organizations or their PACs from subsequently making independent expenditures supporting a particular candidate (or opposing his or her opponent) in amounts above those specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

a. Having contributed in a prior fiscal year or a prior special election cycle to the candidate who would be supported in the expenditure?6

b. Having contributed to the campaign of the candidate for one office (i.e. Senate) in the same fiscal year as an expenditure would be made supporting that candidate for another office (i.e. Lt. Governor, Insurance Commissioner, etc.)?

c. Having contributed to the candidate who would be supported in the expenditure in the same fiscal year or special election cycle as the expenditure would be made?

d. Contracting with a consultant or pollster who in the past, but not in the current fiscal year or special election cycle, has received compensation from the affected candidate or his or her controlled committee?7

6 See In re Bell (1988) 11 FPPC Ops. 1, 18.

7 See 11 CFR section 109.1(b)(4)(i)(B), copy attached. According to Mr. Brad Litchfield, of the Federal Election Commission ("FEC") office of General Counsel, the FEC interprets this provision to permit an independent committee to retain a consultant who is not employed by the candidate in the current federal election cycle but who may have worked for the candidate in the past.

e. Requesting and obtaining any of the following items from agents of the affected candidate's controlled committee (not from the candidate directly):8

1) a glossy photograph(s) of the candidate;

2) a copy of the candidate's official biography?

3) a copy of the candidate's statement distributed to the press in connection with his or her announcement of candidacy?

4) a copy of any other press release distributed by the affected candidate or his or her committee?

5) a copy of the text of speeches made by the affected candidate?

6) a copy of any of the affected candidate's position papers which have been published or distributed to the public?

7) copies of press clippings about the affected candidate?

8) polling data regarding the affected candidate which has been released to the press?

C. Interaction with Parties, Other Candidates, and Other Committees

FACTS

As broad-based committees which contribute to candidates, CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC and California Medical PAC each receive unsolicited materials seeking their support for a particular candidate. Recently, California Medical PAC has received telephone calls from member(s) of the legislature and party representatives about whether it intends to make independent expenditures in support of (or opposition to) a particular candidate(s). CPF received an unsolicited "promotional package" from the Republican legislative leadership

8 In case of non-incumbents, the items listed in 1)-8) may not be available except through the candidate's campaign. CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their sponsored PACs have also requested advice about unsolicited materials. See questions 4 through 8.

in connection with the recent 27th A.D. special election, containing brochures about of the candidate supported by the leadership, biographical, polling, and demographic information, etc. In connection with the run-off in the 27th A.D. (held on January 30, 1990), CPF also received an unsolicited promotional package containing similar materials about the Democratic candidate, with a cover letter from four incumbent assembly members.9 CCPOA-PAC received similar materials, and has also been asked generally about whether or not it intends to make independent expenditures. The general questions have not focused on a particular candidate.

California Medical PAC, CPF-PAC, and CCPOA-PAC have not responded to these inquiries with any information about who (if anyone) they intend to support or oppose through an independent expenditure program. They have declined to talk about the subject. However, each organization and its PAC is concerned that, if they embark upon an independent expenditure program (which always involves the design, printing, mailing, or broadcasting of communications to voters), the independence of the expenditure could be jeopardized by contacts they do not initiate. This could result not only in wasted time and money, but in a frustration of the organization's right to speak out in support of the affected candidate to the degree it wishes.

In light of these facts, and of the language in Government Code section 82031 that an expenditure is independent if it is not made "to or at the behest of the affected candidate," CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC, and California Medical PAC each request advice about what role the legislative leadership and the political parties may play in recommending to the PAC or organization board, or representatives of either, that independent expenditures be made in particular races in support of or opposition to particular candidates.10

QUESTIONS

Question 4: If members of the legislature (including the leadership) or their agents make an unsolicited request of CPF, CMA, CCPOA or their sponsored PACs to make independent

9 Challengers or non-incumbents running in open seats often send these "promotional packages", unsolicited, to likely supporters. See Question 8.

10 The organizations and their PACs presume that they can rely on any information in the public domain, such as newspaper articles, press releases, or other generally disseminated information about which races have been "targeted" by the leadership or the parties.

expenditure(s) for (or against) a specific candidate (other than themselves) are CPF, CCPOA, CMA or their PACs prohibited from subsequently making expenditures for (or against) that candidate in amounts above those specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

Question 5: Would the answer to Question 4 be different if the unsolicited request did not mention "independent expenditures" as such but simply requested "support"?

Question 6: If political party leaders (or their agents) make unsolicited requests (as described in questions 4 and 5), may CCPOA, CMA, CPF or their sponsored PACs subsequently make expenditures for or against the candidate who is the subject of the request in amounts over those prescribed in Government Code section 85303(b)?

Questions 7: If CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their sponsored PACs receive unsolicited "promotional materials" (of the type described in Question 3 c.(1)-(8), see p. 6) from the legislative leadership or the parties, may they use any or all of those materials in their independent expenditure program?

Question 8: If CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their sponsored PACs receive unsolicited "promotional materials" (of the same type as above) from a candidate, may they use any or all of those materials in their independent expenditure program?11

Question 9: Provided no one within any expending committee is acting at the behest of an affected candidate, may CPF, CCPOA, CMA or their sponsored PACs coordinate their expenditures in support of or opposition to an affected candidate with another political committee, broad-based political committee, or person making expenditures for or against the same candidate?

D. Volunteer Activity by Members

FACTS

As large organizations, CCPOA, CPF, and CMA have individual members who independently volunteer their efforts in political campaigns. CCPOA, CMA, and CPF each intend to concentrate decision-making about the nature, content, timing, design, etc. of any independent expenditure at the state board level. However, such a board has no ability to direct and control (or even be informed about) the actions of individual correctional peace officers, firefighters, and doctors within

11 As noted earlier, non-incumbents often send these.

their own districts. CCPOA, CMA, CPF and their PACs are concerned about the possible impact of member volunteer activity in a candidate's campaign on their ability to make independent expenditures for that candidate.

QUESTION

Question 10: Assuming that no individual who participates in the independent expenditure decision-making process is also active in the affected candidate's campaign, if other individual CCPOA, CPF, or CMA members volunteer in the affected candidate's campaign, are CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their PACs prohibited from making expenditures in support of that candidate above the amounts specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

D. Timing and Reporting of Independent Expenditures

FACTS

CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their PACs are concerned that before a mailing or other communication advocating the election of a candidate is sent, circumstances might dictate that the mailer be stopped or the broadcast cancelled. Also, the organizations and their PACs are uncertain at what point in time the independent expenditure notice required by 2 Cal. Code of Regs. section 18564 is "triggered".

QUESTION

Question 11: Has an independent expenditure or in-kind contribution for a mailing or other communication advocating the election of a candidate been "made" for purposes of the limits imposed by Government Code section 85303(c), the notice required by section 85604, or reporting on the allocation schedule of a campaign statement if no communication is actually sent or broadcast?12

E. Appropriateness of Federal Independent Expenditure Guidelines

FACTS

Because 1990 is an election year, CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their PACs will be making imminent decisions about their

12 Of course, all expenditures to vendors made or incurred in connection with the specified mailing would be reportable by the PAC on Schedules E or F of its campaign statement.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan  
March 1, 1990  

independent expenditure programs. Therefore, as a final question they ask the following:

QUESTION

Question 12: While awaiting your response to this letter and in the absence of regulations issued by the Commission, may CPF, CCPOA, CMA and their sponsored PACs look to the attached FEC regulation (11 CFR § 109.1) on independent expenditures and the FEC Advisory Opinions interpreting it for guidance? We would appreciate an expedited response to this final question.

In light of impending special and primary elections, we thank you in advance for your prompt consideration of these particular questions. Please do not hesitate to call me, Chuck Bell, or Chip Nielsen if you need additional material facts or if you need additional time to respond to this request for formal advice.
sections.facts:
CCPOA has a long-established policy and practice that candidates for the same office from each of the two major political parties be interviewed by CCPOA-PAC (the seven Executive Council members and two appointed members-at-large) before any decision to endorse a candidate for that office is made. Candidates are sent letters inviting them to participate in the interview process. CCPOA does not mention possible financial support of any candidate in the invitation letters. The pre-endorsement interviews are usually held shortly after the primary election.2 At the interview, each candidate is asked a series of questions, formulated by the CCPOA Board of Directors (all the elected local chapter presidents), designed to elicit the candidates' views on issues of importance to CCPOA members. No questions are asked regarding the candidate's campaign strategy or campaign funding needs, plans, or projects.

After the interview process, CCPOA-PAC votes on which candidates to endorse. The endorsement of particular candidates is communicated to CCPOA members through The Peacekeeper, CCPOA's monthly magazine. On occasion, a supplemental communication has

1 CCPOA-PAC, California Medical PAC, and CPF-PAC may occasionally make insubstantial contributions to measure committees from PAC funds; however, CMA and CPF usually make such contributions through their sponsored issue committees.

2 In a special election, the candidates are usually interviewed prior to the first of the election dates. Not all candidates may be invited, depending on the nature of the district; however, an attempt is made to invite the leading contenders and to be sure that candidates from both major parties are included.

been sent to members residing in a particular district.3 Following the endorsement process, the CCPOA-PAC board may decide to give financial or other support to an endorsed candidate. In the past, support has been in the form of contributions.

CPF and CMA currently have a less structured candidate interview process.4 However, each is considering establishing a procedure similar to that used by CCPOA. Should they decide to establish a more structured interview procedure, neither CPF nor CMA (or their PAC representatives) would be asking questions of candidates who appear about the candidates' campaign plans, needs, strategy, or projects.

CCPOA, CPF and CMA each strongly believe that interviewing candidates to obtain the candidates' views on issues of significance to their membership is a fundamental to the informed exercise of First Amendment speech and associational rights. The interview is of particular importance as a means of learning where non-incumbents stand, as their views on issues are not usually as widely-known as an incumbent's. However, CCPOA, CPF, and CMA each understands that if a decision is made to make an independent expenditure for a particular candidate following an interview, they may not consult, coordinate, or act in concert with the affected candidate or his or her controlled committee(s) regarding that expenditure.

3 CPF and CMA also have monthly magazines: California Firefighter and California Physician, respectively. CPF, CCPOA, and CMA understand that when independent expenditures are being made in support of a candidate, any simultaneous communications to members regarding an endorsement of the affected candidate also need to be made independently of the candidate. Customarily, the fact that CCPOA has endorsed a candidate has been communicated also to the candidate, usually by a telephone call from a PAC representative. CCPOA is requesting advice regarding whether this practice may continue. See Question No. 2.

4 Both CMA and CPF have informal interview procedures. CPF has delegated the power to endorse to its executive board, which frequently interviews candidates (not necessarily from both parties) in connection with an endorsement. The CPF executive board informs CPF-PAC of the endorsement and CPF-PAC then decides to which endorsed candidates to contribute. Within CMA, candidate interviews are occasionally conducted by the local county medical society, which then communicates the views of the candidates to California Medical PAC and makes contribution recommendations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Question No. 1: Under the circumstances described above, if a candidate accepts an invitation to appear before board representatives of CCPOA, CPF, CMA, or their sponsored PACs and is interviewed exclusively about his or her views on issues of significance to the organization's members (with no discussion of the candidate's campaign plans, projects, or needs), may CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their PACs subsequently make expenditures above the amounts specified in Government Code section 85303(b) in support of or opposition to that candidate, provided there is no consultation, coordination or concerted act between the expending organization (or its PAC) and the affected candidate which occurs subsequent to the interview?5

Question No. 2: If the answer to the above is affirmative, may the interviewing organization also communicate to a candidate who has been endorsed the fact of the endorsement (provided the communication is restricted to conveying that fact), and subsequently make expenditures in support of that candidate greater than the amounts specified in Government Code section 84303(b)?

B. Parameters of Consultation, Coordination, and Concerted Activity

FACTS

CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC, and California Medical PAC have each made contributions in the past to candidates they may now

5 We are aware of technical advice issued to Judge Rutberg (FPPC No. A-87-292) prior to the passage of Proposition 73. Judge Rutberg was advised that expenditures made by an organization by which he had been interviewed were reportable on his campaign statement as contributions received by him, even though Judge Rutberg did not know of the timing, content, or cost of the organization's slate mailer endorsing him (or even that he had been endorsed) until he received a copy of the mailer through regular mail delivery. However, in Rutberg, the organization making the expenditure characterized it as an in-kind contribution in a letter sent to the judge after the election. Judge Rutberg was also informed prior to the interview that a possible slate mailer was "one form of publicity under consideration" if the organization endorsed him. The Rutberg letter appears to assume that by submitting to an interview on issues knowing that a possible expenditure was "under consideration", the candidate and the organization have coordinated the expenditure.

wish to support through an independent expenditures campaign. Each organization is also actively considering retaining its own professional campaign consultant to advise it regarding independent expenditures.

Assume that a decision is made to make independent expenditures (above the amounts prescribed by Government Code section 85303(b)) in support of a particular candidate, either after the interview process described above or without the benefit of that process if the answers to the above questions are negative.

Question No. 3: Do the following actions by CCPOA, CPF, CMA, or their sponsored PACs preclude the organizations or their PACs from subsequently making independent expenditures supporting a particular candidate (or opposing his or her opponent) in amounts above those specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

a. Having contributed in a prior fiscal year or a prior special election cycle to the candidate who would be supported in the expenditure?6

b. Having contributed to the campaign of the candidate for one office (i.e. Senate) in the same fiscal year as an expenditure would be made supporting that candidate for another office (i.e. Lt. Governor, Insurance Commissioner, etc.)?

c. Having contributed to the candidate who would be supported in the expenditure in the same fiscal year or special election cycle as the expenditure would be made?

d. Contracting with a consultant or pollster who in the past, but not in the current fiscal year or special election cycle, has received compensation from the affected candidate or his or her controlled committee?7

6 See In re Bell (1988) 11 FPPC Ops. 1, 18.

7 See 11 CFR section 109.1(b)(4)(i)(B), copy attached. According to Mr. Brad Litchfield, of the Federal Election Commission ("FEC") office of General Counsel, the FEC interprets this provision to permit an independent committee to retain a consultant who is not employed by the candidate in the current federal election cycle but who may have worked for the candidate in the past.

e. Requesting and obtaining any of the following items from agents of the affected candidate's controlled committee (not from the candidate directly):8

1) a glossy photograph(s) of the candidate;

2) a copy of the candidate's official biography?

3) a copy of the candidate's statement distributed to the press in connection with his or her announcement of candidacy?

4) a copy of any other press release distributed by the affected candidate or his or her committee?

5) a copy of the text of speeches made by the affected candidate?

6) a copy of any of the affected candidate's position papers which have been published or distributed to the public?

7) copies of press clippings about the affected candidate?

8) polling data regarding the affected candidate which has been released to the press?

C. Interaction with Parties, Other Candidates, and Other Committees

FACTS

As broad-based committees which contribute to candidates, CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC and California Medical PAC each receive unsolicited materials seeking their support for a particular candidate. Recently, California Medical PAC has received telephone calls from member(s) of the legislature and party representatives about whether it intends to make independent expenditures in support of (or opposition to) a particular candidate(s). CPF received an unsolicited "promotional package" from the Republican legislative leadership

8 In case of non-incumbents, the items listed in 1)-8) may not be available except through the candidate's campaign. CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their sponsored PACs have also requested advice about unsolicited materials. See questions 4 through 8.

in connection with the recent 27th A.D. special election, containing brochures about of the candidate supported by the leadership, biographical, polling, and demographic information, etc. In connection with the run-off in the 27th A.D. (held on January 30, 1990), CPF also received an unsolicited promotional package containing similar materials about the Democratic candidate, with a cover letter from four incumbent assembly members.9 CCPOA-PAC received similar materials, and has also been asked generally about whether or not it intends to make independent expenditures. The general questions have not focused on a particular candidate.

California Medical PAC, CPF-PAC, and CCPOA-PAC have not responded to these inquiries with any information about who (if anyone) they intend to support or oppose through an independent expenditure program. They have declined to talk about the subject. However, each organization and its PAC is concerned that, if they embark upon an independent expenditure program (which always involves the design, printing, mailing, or broadcasting of communications to voters), the independence of the expenditure could be jeopardized by contacts they do not initiate. This could result not only in wasted time and money, but in a frustration of the organization's right to speak out in support of the affected candidate to the degree it wishes.

In light of these facts, and of the language in Government Code section 82031 that an expenditure is independent if it is not made "to or at the behest of the affected candidate," CCPOA-PAC, CPF-PAC, and California Medical PAC each request advice about what role the legislative leadership and the political parties may play in recommending to the PAC or organization board, or representatives of either, that independent expenditures be made in particular races in support of or opposition to particular candidates.10

QUESTIONS

Question 4: If members of the legislature (including the leadership) or their agents make an unsolicited request of CPF, CMA, CCPOA or their sponsored PACs to make independent

9 Challengers or non-incumbents running in open seats often send these "promotional packages", unsolicited, to likely supporters. See Question 8.

10 The organizations and their PACs presume that they can rely on any information in the public domain, such as newspaper articles, press releases, or other generally disseminated information about which races have been "targeted" by the leadership or the parties.

expenditure(s) for (or against) a specific candidate (other than themselves) are CPF, CCPOA, CMA or their PACs prohibited from subsequently making expenditures for (or against) that candidate in amounts above those specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

Question 5: Would the answer to Question 4 be different if the unsolicited request did not mention "independent expenditures" as such but simply requested "support"?

Question 6: If political party leaders (or their agents) make unsolicited requests (as described in questions 4 and 5), may CCPOA, CMA, CPF or their sponsored PACs subsequently make expenditures for or against the candidate who is the subject of the request in amounts over those prescribed in Government Code section 85303(b)?

Questions 7: If CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their sponsored PACs receive unsolicited "promotional materials" (of the type described in Question 3 c.(1)-(8), see p. 6) from the legislative leadership or the parties, may they use any or all of those materials in their independent expenditure program?

Question 8: If CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their sponsored PACs receive unsolicited "promotional materials" (of the same type as above) from a candidate, may they use any or all of those materials in their independent expenditure program?11

Question 9: Provided no one within any expending committee is acting at the behest of an affected candidate, may CPF, CCPOA, CMA or their sponsored PACs coordinate their expenditures in support of or opposition to an affected candidate with another political committee, broad-based political committee, or person making expenditures for or against the same candidate?

D. Volunteer Activity by Members

FACTS

As large organizations, CCPOA, CPF, and CMA have individual members who independently volunteer their efforts in political campaigns. CCPOA, CMA, and CPF each intend to concentrate decision-making about the nature, content, timing, design, etc. of any independent expenditure at the state board level. However, such a board has no ability to direct and control (or even be informed about) the actions of individual correctional peace officers, firefighters, and doctors within

11 As noted earlier, non-incumbents often send these.

their own districts. CCPOA, CMA, CPF and their PACs are concerned about the possible impact of member volunteer activity in a candidate's campaign on their ability to make independent expenditures for that candidate.

QUESTION

Question 10: Assuming that no individual who participates in the independent expenditure decision-making process is also active in the affected candidate's campaign, if other individual CCPOA, CPF, or CMA members volunteer in the affected candidate's campaign, are CCPOA, CPF, CMA or their PACs prohibited from making expenditures in support of that candidate above the amounts specified in Government Code section 85303(b)?

D. Timing and Reporting of Independent Expenditures

FACTS

CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their PACs are concerned that before a mailing or other communication advocating the election of a candidate is sent, circumstances might dictate that the mailer be stopped or the broadcast cancelled. Also, the organizations and their PACs are uncertain at what point in time the independent expenditure notice required by 2 Cal. Code of Regs. section 18564 is "triggered".

QUESTION

Question 11: Has an independent expenditure or in-kind contribution for a mailing or other communication advocating the election of a candidate been "made" for purposes of the limits imposed by Government Code section 85303(c), the notice required by section 85604, or reporting on the allocation schedule of a campaign statement if no communication is actually sent or broadcast?12

E. Appropriateness of Federal Independent Expenditure Guidelines

FACTS

Because 1990 is an election year, CCPOA, CPF, CMA and their PACs will be making imminent decisions about their

12 Of course, all expenditures to vendors made or incurred in connection with the specified mailing would be reportable by the PAC on Schedules E or F of its campaign statement.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan  
March 1, 1990  

independent expenditure programs. Therefore, as a final question they ask the following:

QUESTION

Question 12: While awaiting your response to this letter and in the absence of regulations issued by the Commission, may CPF, CCPOA, CMA and their sponsored PACs look to the attached FEC regulation (11 CFR § 109.1) on independent expenditures and the FEC Advisory Opinions interpreting it for guidance? We would appreciate an expedited response to this final question.

In light of impending special and primary elections, we thank you in advance for your prompt consideration of these particular questions. Please do not hesitate to call me, Chuck Bell, or Chip Nielsen if you need additional material facts or if you need additional time to respond to this request for formal advice.
embedding.first_500_words: **LAW OFFICES OF NIELSEN, MERKSMER, HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER** A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION MARIN COUNTY 591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000 MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941 TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800 SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 770 L STREET, SUITE 800 TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752 SAN FRANCISCO 650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650 SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108 TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800 March 1, 1990 HAND DELIVERED Ms. Kathryn Donovan, General Counsel Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Request for Advice - Independent Expenditures Dear Ms. Donovan: In the past few months, many clients of this firm have asked what guidelines apply to making independent expenditures. We are requesting formal advice pursuant to Government Code section 83114(b) on behalf of three of those clients, each of which is currently considering establishment of its own program for making independent expenditures, as defined in Government Code section 82031. Because the issues about which they are concerned are similar, the following clients have joined in the advice request: • The California Correctional Peace Officers Association ("CCPOA"), a union of state correctional peace officers whose 19,000 members contribute on a voluntary basis to CCPOA's sponsored Political Action Committee ("PAC"), CCPOA-PAC; • California Professional Firefighters ("CPF"), a labor organization comprised of approximately 17,000 professional firefighters, whose members voluntarily contribute to CPF-PAC; • The California Medical Association ("CMA"), a professional association of California physicians, approximately 9,000 of whom have chosen to contribute to California Medical PAC. The committees sponsored by CCPOA, CPF, and CMA are funded by contributions from union or association members. Contributors to CCPOA-PAC and CPF-PAC contribute less than $100 each per fiscal year; most physicians currently contribute less than $100 per fiscal year to California Medical PAC, and none contribute more than $2,500 each per fiscal year. Based on its past activity, each of the sponsored PACs mentioned above has qualified as a "broad-based political committee" (Gov't Code § 85102(d)); each contributes primarily, if not exclusively, to legislative candidates and candidates for statewide office. Neither CCPOA, CPF, nor CMA has in the past made independent expenditures for or against a candidate, through its sponsored PAC or otherwise. However, each organization is currently considering establishment of its own independent expenditure program. Each is familiar with the general guidelines regarding independent expenditures contained in your letter to Assemblyman Richard Mountjoy, FPPC No. I-89-426. CCPOA, CPF, and CMA now request formal advice from the Commission regarding application of the general rules about independent expenditures to the facts stated below. A. Pre-Endorsement Contacts with Candidates FACTS CCPOA has a long-established policy and practice that candidates for the same office from each of the two major political parties be interviewed by CCPOA-PAC (the seven Executive Council members and two appointed members-at-large) before any decision to endorse a candidate for that office is made. Candidates are sent letters inviting them to participate in the interview process. CCPOA does not mention possible financial support of any candidate in the invitation letters. The pre-endorsement interviews are usually held shortly after the primary election.2
